title: Job 3
subtitle: Job Curses the Day of His Birth

heb.paragraph: "Let the day perish on which I was born." Job curses his birthday using the vocabulary of Genesis 1 in reverse. Where God said "Let there be light," Job says "Let that day be darkness." This is anti-creation language — Job wishes his existence undone.

Darkness (ḥōshek̅) is the anti-creation force. In Genesis 1:2, darkness covered the deep before God spoke light. Job wants to return there — to the pre-creation void where he would never have existed.

refs.note: "Let there be light" — the creation language Job inverts.
Jeremiah curses the day of his birth — the closest OT parallel.

notes.note: Job breaks the seven-day silence. His first word is a curse — not against God, but against his own existence. He curses the day and the night.
"Let that day be darkness." He wants creation reversed for the day he was born — let it be swallowed by chaos, erased from the calendar.
"May that night be barren." He wishes the night of his conception had produced nothing. No cry of joy, no new life. Because suffering exists, he wishes existence didn’t.
Calvin: Job does not curse God but curses the day — a distinction the narrator will uphold. The despair is real but the line is not crossed.
Calvin: wishing the day were darkness is wishing creation undone. Only extreme suffering produces this kind of theological vertigo.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes cursing one’s birthday is attested in Mesopotamian literature. Job’s lament draws on a shared ANE tradition of extreme grief expression.
The NET identifies "those who curse the day" (or "rouse Leviathan") as sorcerers believed to have power over cosmic forces — Job invokes even mythological chaos against his own existence.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the anti-creation motif is deliberate and systematic. Job uses the vocabulary of Genesis 1 (light, darkness, day, night, sea) in reverse order. He is not merely upset; he is theologically deconstructing his own existence.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Job’s opening speech is a legal motion to annul his birth. He is not arguing his case yet — he is establishing that his suffering is so great that existence itself is the problem.

hist.context: After seven days of silence, Job’s first words are not a prayer, not an accusation against God, and not a question about why. They are a wish to have never been born. The speech is the opposite of Genesis 1: systematic un-creation. Day by day, element by element, Job takes apart the world that produced him.

ane.similarity: Both texts structure the theodicy question as a dialogue between a sufferer and a friend who defends divine justice. Both use the back-and-forth format to explore whether the gods reward righteousness and punish wickedness. The Babylonian text's acrostic form shows the same literary sophistication as Job's poetic structure.
ane.difference: The Babylonian friend eventually concedes that the gods made humans inherently prone to wickedness — a pessimistic anthropology. Job's friends never concede; they maintain that suffering proves guilt until Yahweh himself refutes them. Job's resolution lies not in anthropology but in theophany.
ane.significance: The dialogue form for exploring theodicy was established ANE practice. Job's contribution is the divine speech that bypasses the theodicy question entirely — Yahweh neither explains nor apologizes but overwhelms Job with cosmic reality beyond human categories.

ane.parallel: A Man and His God (Sumerian, c. 2000 BCE)
ane.similarity: This earliest known righteous sufferer text features complaint, protestation of innocence, and eventual divine restoration — the same arc as Job's prose frame. The Sumerian sufferer, like Job, describes physical affliction, social abandonment, and desperate appeal to his deity.
ane.difference: The Sumerian sufferer eventually confesses that all humans sin, and his restoration follows confession. Job never confesses guilt for sins he did not commit; his 'repentance' in chapter 42 is better translated as 'relenting' — he stops demanding answers, but he never admits the fault his friends imputed.
ane.significance: The Sumerian text shows that the righteous sufferer genre is among humanity's oldest literary forms. Job stands in this tradition but radicalizes it: the protagonist refuses the conventional confession-and-restoration resolution.

sections.header: Verses 11–26 — "Why Did I Not Die at Birth?" — Death as Rest

heb.paragraph: "Why did I not perish at birth?" (lāmâ). The question is repeated five times in the chapter (vv.11, 12, 16, 20, 23). It is not a request for information but a cry of protest. Job does not ask "why do I suffer?" but "why do I exist?"

In death, Job says, "I would be at rest" (nûaḥ). Kings, counsellors, prisoners — all are equal in death. The grave is the great equaliser. This is not nihilism but longing: death is described as the rest that life denies.

refs.note: "I declared that the dead are happier than the living" — the same grim calculus.
Paul’s "to depart and be with Christ is far better" — the Christian reframing of Job’s longing.

notes.note: Stillborn children are envied: "I would be lying down in peace; I would be asleep and at rest." Death is portrayed as the sleep the living are denied.
"There the wicked cease from turmoil, and the weary are at rest." The democracy of death: slave and master, prisoner and guard — all equal.

"Why is light given to those in misery?" Now God is implicitly accused: he gives life to those who long for death. The "hedge" of v.23 inverts 1:10 — the Satan said God hedged Job with blessing; now Job says God hedges him with suffering.
"What I feared has come upon me." Job’s final words in the speech. The silence is broken, the debate is opened, the friends now have something to respond to.
Calvin: Job does not accuse God directly but asks "why?" — which is the beginning of accusation. The line between lament and complaint is thin, and Job walks it.
Calvin: the triple negative — no peace, no quietness, no rest — is the cry of a soul that has lost every foothold. This is the darkest point before the debate begins.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET connects "hedged in" to 1:10 where the Satan uses the same verb. The ironic reversal is intentional: protection becomes prison.
The NET notes the chapter ends abruptly with no resolution — a deliberate literary choice that forces the reader forward into the debate.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the inverted hedge is the speech’s sharpest irony. In 1:10 the Satan complained that God’s hedge protected Job too well. Now Job complains the hedge imprisons him. Same word, opposite meaning.
Clines on "I have no peace, no quietness, no rest" — the triple negative is the antithesis of the rest Job envied in vv.13–19. He can neither live nor die, neither rest nor escape.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: the speech functions as the opening statement of the plaintiff. Job has established his standing (blameless), his suffering (total), and his protest (existence itself is the problem). The trial can begin.

hist.context: The speech moves from wishing he’d never been born (vv.1–10) to wishing he’d died at birth (vv.11–19) to asking why God gives life to the suffering (vv.20–26). The trajectory narrows: from cosmic protest to personal accusation. By v.23, God is the implied target: "Why is light given to a man whose way is hidden, whom God has hedged in?"

ppl.name: Job
ppl.text: After seven days of silence, Job’s first words are not a prayer, not an accusation against God, and not a question about why…

ppl.name: Satan
ppl.text: 23 inverts 1:10 — the Satan said God hedged Job with blessing…

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: After seven days of silence, Job’s first words are not a prayer, not an accusation against God, and not a question about why…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThat day—may it turn to darkness; may God above not care about it; may no light shine on it.ESVLet that day be darkness! May God above not seek it, nor light shine upon it.NIV“May the day of my birth perish, and the night that said, ‘A boy is conceived!’ESV“Let the day perish on which I was born, and the night that said, ‘A man is conceived.’

rec.text: "Job breaks the seven-day silence. His first word is a curse — not against God, but against his own existence. He curses the day and the night."

rows.label: Death Envied — 3:11–19
rows.text: The stillborn are blessed. The grave is rest. The great equaliser.

rows.label: The Question — 3:20–26
rows.text: "Why is light given to the miserable?" The hedge inverted. No peace, no rest.

Darkness (ḥōshek̅) is the anti-creation force. In Genesis 1:2, darkness covered the deep before God spoke light. Job wants to return there — to the pre-creation void where he would never have existed.

themes.note: Justice dominates: Job’s protest is fundamentally about fairness — why give life to the suffering? Sovereignty: God is the implied cause.
